planting.note: Sow in late summer 6–10 weeks before first fall frost, or in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; Mammoth is a single-cut, late-maturing type of red clover that produces the largest plant mass and highest nitrogen fixation of the standard red clovers.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed at 10–12 lbs per acre (1 lb per 1,000 sq ft) onto a firm, weed-free seedbed; inoculate with clover rhizobia for reliable nitrogen fixation in new ground. Mammoth Red Clover is a large, late-maturing single-cut type that produces more biomass per planting than standard medium red clover, reaching 3–4 feet in height and fixing 80–200 lbs of nitrogen per acre when properly inoculated; it is best terminated at early bloom for maximum nitrogen availability to the following crop. The large, showy magenta flower heads are outstanding for pollinators — a diverse range of native bees, bumble bees, and honey bees work the blooms heavily. In the South, sow in fall as a winter annual that overwinters, blooms in spring, and is terminated before summer cash crops go in.

planting.note: Sow in late summer 6–10 weeks before first fall frost for a frost-killed winter cover in zones 4–8; the oat plant is killed by temperatures below 20°F, making it a convenient 'mow-free' winter cover that lies flat and decomposes naturally without spring tillage.

directions: Broadcast or drill seed at 2–3 oz per 100 sq ft onto a well-prepared, lightly raked bed in late summer or early fall; germination is rapid in cool soil and the oat establishes quickly, outcompeting late-season weeds. Spring oat is one of the most versatile and widely used cool-season cover crops because it is killed by hard frost (below 20°F) without any action by the gardener, leaving a mat of dead mulch that protects bare soil through winter and decomposes quickly in spring with minimal residue management; it also mixes exceptionally well with hairy vetch or crimson clover, providing a physical trellis for the climbing legume while the legume fixes nitrogen. For warm climates (zones 8–11) sow in early spring as a short-term summer smother crop between vegetable beds.

planting.note: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost when soil has warmed above 60°F; a vigorous pole bean that needs a trellis or fence 5–6 feet tall and is harvested as a dry bean in fall after the pods have dried on the vine.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep after all frost danger has passed into fertile, well-drained soil against a tall trellis; do not soak beans before planting as it can cause the seed coat to split. Good Mother Stallard is an heirloom pole bean introduced through Seed Savers Exchange with beautiful, plum-purple and white mottled pods and seeds that develop into a creamy, meaty dry bean with a remarkably rich, complex, steak-like flavor when cooked — widely considered one of the best-flavored dry beans available to home gardeners. Allow pods to mature and dry completely on the vine before harvesting; if frost threatens, pull the entire vine and hang in a protected location to finish drying. Store shelled dry beans in a cool, airtight container for up to a year.

planting.note: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost once soil warms above 60°F; a Native American heirloom dry pole bean from the Hidatsa people of the northern Great Plains, traditionally grown in the Three Sisters system with corn and squash; plant at the base of corn stalks for traditional companion planting.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm soil after last frost, spacing 6 inches apart along a trellis or the stalks of sweet corn in the traditional Three Sisters companion planting arrangement. Hidatsa Red is a Native American heirloom dry pole bean from the Hidatsa people of the upper Missouri River region (present-day North Dakota), with deep maroon-red seeds that cook to a creamy, full-bodied texture with an earthy, robust flavor well-suited to soups and stews; the variety was collected and preserved through the work of Alfred Mandan and introduced more widely by Seed Savers Exchange. Allow pods to ripen and dry completely on the vine; pods are tan with dark mottling when ready. An important cultural heritage bean that rewards the grower with both excellent eating and connection to a long agricultural tradition.

planting.note: Direct sow 1 inch deep after last frost once soil has warmed; a compact bush type requiring no trellis; one of the earlier-maturing heirloom dry beans, well-suited to shorter seasons and successive plantings.

directions: Sow seed 1 inch deep in warm, well-drained soil after last frost, spacing 4–6 inches apart in rows 18 inches apart; Marfax germinates quickly in warm soil and the compact bush plants require no staking or support. Marfax is a New England heirloom bush dry bean with attractive tan-and-brown mottled seeds that are smaller and rounder than kidney-type beans and have a creamy, buttery texture when cooked with a mild, nutty flavor excellent in baked beans, soups, and salads. Allow pods to fully mature and begin to yellow and rattle before harvesting; if a wet spell threatens, pull plants and hang them under cover to finish drying. Marfax stores well for a year or more when dried thoroughly and kept in an airtight container at room temperature.
